pr.title: Introduce distributed transaction coordinator with two-phase commit
pr.author: priya-nkosi
pr.description:
Adds a `TransactionCoordinator` that orchestrates two-phase commit (2PC) across multiple resource managers. This is needed to support the upcoming cross-shard write path for the new billing engine.

The coordinator tracks participant state in Redis, handles prepare/commit/abort phases, and exposes a context-manager API for callers.

This is a large change and I'm aware there are open questions around failure-recovery semantics — leaving those as TODOs for now and hoping the panel can weigh in.

concerns: Redis is used as the coordinator log — if Redis is compromised or unavailable mid-transaction, partial commits may silently corrupt billing state.
No mention of encryption or access control on the coordinator log keys.
The `abort` path does not appear to emit an audit event, breaking the billing audit trail.
praise: Good use of UUIDs for transaction IDs — avoids enumeration.

concerns: Two-phase commit adds at least two round-trips per write; at projected billing volume this could add 40-80 ms p99 latency on the write path.
The coordinator holds a Redis lock for the full duration of the prepare phase — this could serialize writes under contention.
No benchmarks or load-test results included.
praise: Async participant notifications are a good call — avoids blocking the coordinator thread.

concerns: The TODOs around failure-recovery semantics are load-bearing: if the coordinator crashes after prepare but before commit, there is no documented recovery path.
No dead-letter or timeout handling for participants that never respond to prepare.
Tests for the recovery module are present but all marked `@pytest.mark.skip` with no explanation.
praise: Transaction log structure is well-designed and should support replay-based recovery once implemented.

concerns: The coordinator and participant are tightly coupled through a shared Redis schema with no versioning strategy.
Several magic timeout constants are inlined; these should be config-driven.
The architecture doc is a good start but doesn't cover the failure modes discussed in concerns above.
praise: Context-manager API is ergonomic and will be easy for callers to adopt.
